comments:
Summary
The manuscript proposes hypernetworks: smaller networks that generate weights for larger “main” networks. Two settings are explored: static hypernetworks that generate convolutional filters for deep convnets (e.g. residual networks), and dynamic hypernetworks (HyperRNN/HyperLSTM) that produce timestep- and sample-dependent weight-scaling vectors for recurrent models. The methods are evaluated on multiple tasks (MNIST, CIFAR-10, character-level Penn Treebank, enwik8, and handwriting generation) and show competitive or state-of-the-art results on several language and handwriting tasks while reducing parameter counts in some convnet experiments.

Strengths
- Broad coverage: the paper explores both static and dynamic hypernetworks and applies them to vision and sequence tasks, demonstrating the method’s generality.
- Empirical results: experiments across diverse datasets (including enwik8 and handwriting) with comparisons to relevant baselines (LSTM, Layer Norm, dropout, etc.) support the claims that hypernetworks can improve performance or parameter efficiency in many cases.
- Practical considerations: the authors propose memory-efficient variants (row-wise scaling) making the approach scalable for RNNs, and discuss integration with normalization techniques.
- Original idea and appealing motivation: framing hypernetworks as relaxed weight-sharing and the genotype/phenotype analogy is compelling.

Weaknesses / Points for improvement
- Missing details and clarity in places: the provided text references tables and figures (e.g. quantitative tables for CIFAR-10, MNIST, PTB) that are not shown here; some reported numbers and specifics of architectures and training schedules are summarized but full clarity is limited in the excerpt. For reproducibility the paper should include explicit architectures, exact hyperparameters, full table values, and training times.
- Ablation and analysis: while several experiments are presented, more ablation studies would strengthen the paper. In particular: sensitivity to embedding size Nz and hypernetwork capacity; impact of using Wout shared vs. per-layer outputs; quantitative runtime and memory overhead vs. benefits; and how performance scales with dataset/model size.
- Comparisons: the paper compares to reasonable baselines (LSTM, Layer Norm LSTM, wide ResNets), but comparisons to other parameter-reduction or dynamic-weight approaches (e.g., low-rank factorization, conditional/mixture-of-experts style parameterizations) are limited or absent in the presented text. A clearer positioning relative to related alternatives would help.
- Interpretability and insight: the paper offers some visualizations showing when the hypernetwork changes weights, and histograms of activations, but more analysis on why hypernetworks help (what patterns they encode, whether they learn interpretable layer embeddings) would increase the work’s scientific value.
- Presentation artifacts: in the supplied manuscript text there are formatting glitches and missing table/figure contents which impede reading. The final version should ensure all figures/tables and captions are present and readable.

Recommendations to Authors
1) Add comprehensive ablations: vary Nz, hypernetwork depth/width, and the decision to share Wout to show which design choices matter. 2) Report computational costs: training time, inference latency, and peak memory overhead relative to baselines. 3) Include full experimental tables and hyperparameter lists to aid reproducibility; consider releasing code and model checkpoints. 4) Expand analysis: probe what layer embeddings encode, visualize generated filters more extensively, and analyze failure cases. 5) Improve presentation: fix formatting, ensure all referenced figures/tables are included and legible, and tighten exposition in the methods section (equations and notation). 

Verdict
This is an original, well-motivated, and empirically substantial paper that introduces a flexible approach to generate network weights and demonstrates benefits across multiple tasks. With more detailed ablations, runtime/memory analysis, and improved presentation, it would be an excellent contribution. I recommend acceptance and an oral presentation.
